paragraphs.text: Federal Centre for Child and Youth Media Protection
KidD. pbund.de Child Rights Enforcement Body in Digital Services

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: The body established in Germany for the enforcement of children's rights in digital services (Kidd) requires digital service providers to comply with statutory provisions on the basis of the Digital Services Act (DSA) and the German Youth Protection Act (JuSchG). It is set up as an independent body at the Federal Centre for Child and Youth Media Protection (BzKJ).

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: This opinion is based on Kidd's practical experience and refers in particular to the protection of children and young people's media.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Kidd welcomes the European Commission's initiative to adapt European consumer law to the challenges of the digital world. In particular, it is welcome to focus on minors who are considered to be particularly vulnerable because of their inexperience in commercial transactions. The UN Convention on the Rights of the Child and the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights also make this priority based on the 'best interest of the child' principle necessary in view of the potentially extraordinary disadvantages. The current regulatory framework does not provide sufficient protection that respects the group of children and young people as 'vulnerable consumers' with specific risk situations. This is particularly relevant taking into account the vulnerability of children, in line with UN General Comment No 25 on the rights of children in the digital environment.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Practical impressions on "unfair commercial practices and unfair designs" of digital services

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Kidd agrees with the Commission Staff Working Document Fitness Check on EU consumer law on digital fairness as far as the presentation of unfair practices is concerned and can confirm a large number of observations on unfair designs from its own practice. This includes the following, without limit:

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Loss of personal autonomy and risk to personal integrity

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Children and young people have little business experience and are particularly dependent on digital services, as today a large proportion of childhood and youth are digital. Social participation of minors is thus linked to their own participation in digital services. Social networks are also a popular leisure activity among minors. According to the 2024 KIM study (available at: https://mpfs.de/studie/kim-studie-2024/) are the top five apps of Messenger WhatsApp, as well as social networks YouTube, TikTok, Instagram and Snapchat.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Depending on the nature of the services, these present not only opportunities but also significant risks. It is difficult to understand content, interactions and resulting dynamics. For example, severe disadvantages can result from use, such as cybergrooming, cyberbullying, excessive use and many others. The working methods used often encourage problematic media use behaviour, in particular through push alerts, algorithmic

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: recommendations, endless feeds and other data-driven practices that can continuously become 'unfair' with the increasing collection of consumer data. With the power of algorithms and other tools on social media, young participants in the market – through targeted intellectual foreign supply – can thus face a loss of autonomy, ownership and community capacity, and can have a lasting negative impact on life, and in particular on consumer behaviour. The increasing use of artificial intelligence (AI), with its complexity and opacity, will add to the vulnerability situation for underage consumers. In particular, the possibility of emotion recognition AI and anthropomorphic AI systems suggests such presumptions. AI chatbots, in particular, have a particular risk potential.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: For children and adolescents, play platforms are also an important leisure and meeting place. Minors often spend several hours per day on these platforms.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: The addictive potential of digital products and services poses a risk of economic, physical and psychological harm, including and especially for vulnerable consumers such as children. While most of the problematic design features already existed in 2017, both market size and consumer use of digital products, social media and video games have increased in the EU. In serious cases, in addition to or accompanying excessive use behaviour, such fatal consequences for minors may arise, such as slipping into extremism, loss of democratic capacity, disregard for sexual self-determination, self-harming behaviour to suicide, life-threatening 'challenges', mental health problems such as depression, fascination for life-threatening courage, examples of many other phenomena.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Loss of economic autonomy, in particular through unfair business practices in computer games as a relevant area of 'unfair design'

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: The loss of autonomy due to "unfair digital media" can happen at many levels. A particularly important level in the context of lack of fairness is also the level of economic autonomy. An example of this is influencers. These are regularly important advertisements which do not always indicate in a transparent manner the commercial nature of the content shared. In this way, the trust of participants in the market can be shaken or gained unfairly and the autonomy of minors can be compromised in many ways.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: A variety of unfair commercial practices can also be observed on gaming platforms. Digital games are increasingly widespread and are among the most popular leisure activities of minors. According to the KIM study, 64% of all minors play at least weekly and 28% even daily. In doing so, they come into contact with the various monetisation models of digital games. So-called loot boxes are considered to be particularly problematic. These are virtual containers containing random content. Content varies in its usefulness and rarity and is an integral part of many successful games. Loot boxes can regularly be 'played' or purchased as a result of the progress of the game.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Players thus have the possibility to acquire virtual content through real money, the exact content of which is random. Whether individual configurations of loot boxes constitute games of chance in the technical sense must

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: PAGE 2 of 8

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: be determined by the competent supervisory authorities. In the case of loot boxes, similar characteristics are regularly available in computer games or on their gambling platforms. Minors usually do not have the necessary business experience and sufficient impulse control to reflect critically the risks and mechanisms associated with loot boxes.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Video games and game platforms have thus increasingly become an online marketplace for minors. The use of purchases in play and virtual objects such as loot boxes can lead to unfair designs. In addition, virtual currencies make it difficult or impossible to understand the actual price of a virtual item. Young market participants may be overwhelmed, especially when converting them.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
4. Other threats to children and adolescents through typical mechanisms
paragraphs.text: The possibilities of unfair commercial practices are diverse and can only be illustrated in the context of this opinion. In essence, however, similar mechanisms can be identified. In particular:

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
4. Other threats to children and adolescents through typical mechanisms
paragraphs.text: Today, the need for social participation is particularly evident in the digital sphere. The resulting reliance on digital services can lead to a delicate and unfair imbalance between service providers and users, depending on the conditions of use.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
4. Other threats to children and adolescents through typical mechanisms
paragraphs.text: A digital environment that encourages the exploitation of young market participants' inexperience and ongoing personal development in a variety of forms, such as obscuring advertising, various deceptions and manipulative designs, unfair interaction models, or exploiting the inexperience through the collection of consumer data.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
4. Other threats to children and adolescents through typical mechanisms
paragraphs.text: Objectively difficult to understand, subdivide, e.g. potentially addictive functionalities, such as elements similar to games of chance, such as loot boxes.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
4. Other threats to children and adolescents through typical mechanisms
paragraphs.text: To illustrate these and many other problem areas that may arise in the context of unfair designs, we refer to the 'atlas of danger' published by the BzKJ. This is available here:

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
4. Other threats to children and adolescents through typical mechanisms
paragraphs.text: https://www.bzkj.de/resource/blob/197826/5e88ec66e545bcb196b7bf81fc6dd9e3/2-auflage-atlas-data.pdf

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
II. Key actions to tackle unfair designs
paragraphs.text: From our broad practical observations, key points of view can be drawn on how effectively unfair designs should be addressed on digital services in order to effectively protect minors.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
II. Key actions to tackle unfair designs
paragraphs.text: A variety of points of approach can be identified to safely address unfair designs. Three main themes are presented below:

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
1. Mandatory age verification
paragraphs.text: In the offline world, age checks on age-restricted goods and services are standard. It is therefore essential to expect similar protective measures also in the case of online offers with significant potential risks, especially for

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
1. Mandatory age verification
paragraphs.text: PAGE 3 of 8

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
1. Mandatory age verification
paragraphs.text: minors. Consequently, age verification is meaningful, necessary, appropriate and proportionate.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
1. Mandatory age verification
paragraphs.text: Age verification is a key tool to protect minors from harmful content and dangerous interactions. They also provide the basis for secure default settings. They can also contribute to safe digital spaces for minors, where adults are exemplarily excluded from use.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
1. Mandatory age verification
paragraphs.text: The Kidd therefore welcomes the fact that the European Commission, on the basis of Article 28 of the DSA, issued guidelines in July 2025 that specify the obligations of online platform providers, in particular with regard to age verification. They provide, among other things, for providers to check the age of the users when appropriate risks to their privacy, security and protection so require. As a result, suitable safe defaults shall be used and exploitative practices shall be avoided.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
1. Mandatory age verification
paragraphs.text: However, Article 28 DSA only obliges providers of 'online platforms'. However, age verification should in principle be based on existing risks for children and adolescents, so the protection should be extended, in particular with regard to the provision of Article 19 of the DSA, where it is an offer where age verification can make a significant contribution to risk minimisation.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
1. Mandatory age verification
paragraphs.text: It is true that significant risks for children and young people can also come from online platforms that justify the assessment as small and micro-enterprises. These services must also be able to provide adequate protection from the point of view of safeguarding children's rights and ensuring an adequate level of protection in the event of serious risks on the basis of an individual risk assessment and meeting analysis.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
1. Mandatory age verification
paragraphs.text: Given the importance of reliable age verification for the protection of minors' media, the current level of protection does not seem sufficient. Effective and widespread age verification is one of the key tools to mitigate the negative effects of illegal and age-inappropriate content, harmful business practices, addictive or manipulating design elements and excessive data collection. This is why we call for mandatory age verification for digital services in clearly defined cases deriving from the 'unfair' problems mentioned above.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
2. Enforcing age verification and age limits on social networks
paragraphs.text: As of now, social network providers are insufficiently reviewing the existing age limits. There is an urgent need for action on the part of the provider in order to effectively enforce age restrictions, which currently often result from the conditions of use, in order to make safe defaults fully effective.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
2. Enforcing age verification and age limits on social networks
paragraphs.text: In addition to the aforementioned aspects, the need for functional age checks stems in particular from national and European considerations on the introduction of a minimum age for the use of social networks. In order to be coordinated across the EU, the discussion on the introduction of a minimum social media age should take place within the framework of EU legislation.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
2. Enforcing age verification and age limits on social networks
paragraphs.text: The question of an appropriate minimum age must take into account age-appropriate participation prospects. Modern protection for minors always lies in the tension between protection, empowerment and participation.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
2. Enforcing age verification and age limits on social networks
paragraphs.text: PAGE 4 of 8

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
2. Enforcing age verification and age limits on social networks
paragraphs.text: Kidd welcomes the establishment of a Commission of Experts on this issue.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
2. Enforcing age verification and age limits on social networks
paragraphs.text: Against this background, the Kidd considers that the European Commission is competent to assess whether setting a minimum age for social networks under the Digital Fairness Act is appropriate and proportionate, taking into account existing participatory aspects.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
3. Problematic monetisation models in gaming
paragraphs.text: For the reasons set out above, the Kidd also advocates uniform regulation of problematic monetisation models in digital games. In addition, pay-to-win systems, battle passes or digital game currencies, for example, should not be lost out of sight. The time limitation of certain offers is also seen as critical, as it generates pressure and influences users in their purchasing decisions.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
3. Problematic monetisation models in gaming
paragraphs.text: From a child law perspective, a basic and clear legal provision is needed, together with a clear strategy on how to deal with loot boxes and other simulated gambling and adjacent phenomena, even if the gaming platform does not meet the requirements of an online platform within the meaning of the DSA.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
3. Problematic monetisation models in gaming
paragraphs.text: At the same time, clear age targets and age markings, together with age verification, are very important in this context, as are the preservation of parental control.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
3. Problematic monetisation models in gaming
paragraphs.text: Germany has a successful system of regulated self-regulation. Important best practice approaches can be derived from decades of practice and the basic system. For example, in the context of age marking by self-monitoring institutions under the national law on the protection of minors, existing risks and risk-matching strategies are taken into account through preventive measures in order to obtain a guidance and robust age label. In addition to the purely substantive assessment, the associated risks include, in particular, interaction risks and risks of use such as chats or purchase incentives. In addition to the actual age mark, appropriate descriptors are used to show the risks.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
3. Problematic monetisation models in gaming
paragraphs.text: III The need for a comprehensive strategic approach on "fairness by design" and clear safeguards against "unfair practices"

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
3. Problematic monetisation models in gaming
paragraphs.text: Regulatory complexity has increased as the new digital legislation needs to be applied alongside EU consumer law in parallel enforcement structures.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
3. Problematic monetisation models in gaming
paragraphs.text: This complexity should be reduced in terms of fairness. The protection of children and young people's media requires a transparent, fully uniform regulatory approach in order to remain enforceable.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
3. Problematic monetisation models in gaming
paragraphs.text: This approach should also include the introduction of a "fairness by design" obligation for platform providers, which would include technical and organisational measures to integrate consumer protection considerations into all stages of product or service development. The scope of such an obligation should cover the commercial path of the consumer from advertising to the after-sales stage. This obligation must also be tailored to the needs of children and young people.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
3. Problematic monetisation models in gaming
paragraphs.text: PAGE 5 of 8

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
3. Problematic monetisation models in gaming
paragraphs.text: In order to implement these approaches, it should be examined whether additions can also be made to Article 28 of the DSA in order to address the risk of further fragmentation in the protection of children and young people's media, given that the solutions presented correspond to the structural protection approach set out in Article 28(1) DSA.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
3. Problematic monetisation models in gaming
paragraphs.text: Irrespective of the specific location of the envisaged rules, the aspects of combating unfair commercial practices should be formulated not only in recommendations but in directly enforceable standards. Restrictions on obligations such as the provisions on small and micro-enterprises in Article 19 of the DSA should be reconsidered as a matter of urgency, as the link to staff and turnover figures does not necessarily allow for robust statements on the relevance of a platform and its utility figures and, as a result, legal-free spaces with particular risk potential are created.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
3. Problematic monetisation models in gaming
paragraphs.text: Particular attention should be paid to consistency with the DSA in the further development of the DFA, in order to formulate uniform requirements for suppliers and enable effective enforcement. In addition, best practice approaches from Member States should be taken into account in order to integrate best practices into consumer protection.

section_path: Opinion of the Body on the Enforcement of Children's Rights in Digital Services on the Digital Fairness Act
3. Problematic monetisation models in gaming
